text: The video shows a robotic cholecystectomy with visible omental attachments and a large, tense gallbladder filled with stones. The gallbladder has been aspirated, but little fluid was removed due to the stones. The patient's liver is notably large, with the left hepatic lobe extending across to the right side, covering the gallbladder infundibulum.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to perform a cholecystectomy on a patient with acute cholecystitis. The gallbladder appears tense and full of stones, making it difficult to grasp. The surgeon begins by dissecting the peritoneum on the lateral aspect, moving towards the medial aspect, and stripping down the thick, inflamed peritoneum. The Firefly mode with ICG is utilized to visualize cystic duct structures, aiding in the identification of the common bile duct and duodenum amidst the challenging tissue.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to dissect the cystic artery free from the nearby cystic duct in the inflamed triangle of Calot. The Firefly mode aids in identifying the cystic duct amidst the inflammatory tissue, switching between white light and Firefly mode for better visualization. The gallbladder infundibulum is grasped to facilitate dissection in the context of acute cholecystitis.

The surgeon uses scissors to dissect around the cystic duct and cystic artery, clearing inflammatory tissue to expose the first third of the hepatic cystic plate. Firefly imaging aids in identifying the cystic duct, ensuring accurate placement of clips amidst dense inflammation. Two clips are placed on the cystic duct and one on the cystic artery.

The surgeon ligates and cuts the cystic duct, followed by the cystic artery, using monopolar energy through scissors to detach the inflamed gallbladder from the liver. Smoke evacuation is utilized due to extensive electrocautery.
The surgeon finalizes the dissection, detaching the gallbladder from the liver. The gallbladder, visibly tense and filled with stones, is placed into a specimen bag for removal.
